content: Most innovation failures are not execution failures — they are framing failures. Teams invest enormous energy solving the wrong problem with great precision. The highest-leverage moment in any innovation process is not when you pick the best solution, but when you decide which question is actually worth answering.
implication: Before any ideation session, teams should invest deliberate time auditing whether they are working on the right problem. A well-framed question is not overhead — it is the most valuable work product the team will produce.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: The 'How Might We' framing is not a warm-up exercise — it is a precision instrument. Crafting the right question before generating any solutions is the single highest-leverage skill in the innovation toolkit. A narrow question produces incremental answers; a reframed question can open entirely new solution spaces that the original framing made invisible.
implication: Facilitators and team leads should treat question-crafting as a standalone work session, not a preamble to brainstorming. Spend as much time sharpening the 'How Might We' as you plan to spend on generating answers to it.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar — Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care?

content: Sitting with a problem — resisting the gravitational pull toward solutions — is an act of discipline, not passivity. Utley frames the discomfort of the open question as productive, because premature closure on a solution is one of the most common and costly mistakes teams make. The willingness to stay in the question space longer than feels comfortable is what separates genuine breakthroughs from incremental improvements.
implication: Leaders should explicitly name and protect the problem-exploration phase in meetings. When someone jumps to solutions, the facilitator's job is to redirect back to the question — not because the solution is wrong, but because the question may not yet be right.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Unconventional Paths to Breakthroughs — video

content: The ability to find the right problem is rarer and more valuable than the ability to solve problems. Solvers are abundant; problem finders are scarce. Most professional training — in business schools, engineering programs, and corporate development — focuses almost entirely on solution generation, leaving the prior and more critical skill of problem identification largely unaddressed.
implication: Organizations that want genuine innovation should invest in training people to find better problems, not just generate more solutions. Problem-finding workshops, reframing exercises, and structured question audits are as important as any ideation methodology.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: Utley draws a sharp distinction between working on the right problem and working on the problem right. Most organizations are extraordinarily competent at the latter — optimizing, executing, refining — and dramatically underpowered at the former. Efficiency applied to the wrong problem is not just wasteful; it actively crowds out the time and attention needed to find better problems.
implication: Build a regular organizational ritual — quarterly, at minimum — dedicated to questioning whether the problems currently on the roadmap are actually the highest-value problems. Execution excellence should never substitute for problem-selection rigor.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Reframing a problem from multiple angles before committing to a solution direction is not indecision — it is the creative act itself. Utley teaches that a problem stated from a different vantage point often reveals solutions that were entirely invisible from the original framing. The skill of holding multiple framings simultaneously, rather than locking onto the first one, is what distinguishes expert innovators from novices.
implication: Build a 'reframe round' into every problem-solving session: before generating solutions, require the team to articulate the problem from at least three different perspectives — the customer's, the competitor's, and the skeptic's — and notice where the framings diverge.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar — Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care?

content: Design Thinking, as taught at the Stanford d.school, places empathy and problem definition ahead of ideation for a structural reason: you cannot generate solutions to a problem you do not yet understand. The 'define' phase — where teams synthesize observations into a precise, human-centered problem statement — is not a bureaucratic checkpoint but the creative core of the entire process.
implication: Teams that skip directly to brainstorming after a brief user research phase are likely optimizing a problem statement that was never stress-tested. Build in explicit synthesis sessions where raw observations are turned into crisp problem statements before any solution generation begins.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar — Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care?

content: Utley identifies a consistent pattern: the teams that generate the most breakthrough ideas are not the ones with the highest IQ or the most experience — they are the ones who spent the most time upstream, in the question space, before generating solutions. Time invested in problem articulation is not a cost; it is a multiplier on everything that follows.
implication: When a team feels stuck in ideation, the answer is rarely to brainstorm harder. The answer is almost always to go back upstream and ask whether the problem is framed correctly. Better questions unlock better ideas faster than any ideation technique.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley — podcast

content: One of Utley's core teaching moves is distinguishing between a symptom and a root problem. What teams present as the problem to be solved is frequently a surface manifestation of a deeper, more important problem. Training people to ask 'is this actually the problem, or a symptom of the problem?' is one of the highest-value interventions in any innovation curriculum.
implication: Add a 'five whys' or root-cause interrogation step before finalizing any problem statement. If the team cannot articulate why solving this problem matters beyond the immediate surface, they have likely not yet found the real problem worth solving.
source_ref: The Reason You Don't Have Better Ideas — video

content: Utley argues that AI can be a powerful partner in the problem-finding phase — not just the solution-generation phase. When given a rich problem context, AI can help surface adjacent framings, challenge the assumptions embedded in the original question, and generate alternative 'How Might We' statements that human teams would not naturally produce. The bottleneck is whether people think to use AI upstream, before solutions, rather than only downstream.
implication: Experiment with using AI at the start of a project to interrogate the problem framing: ask it to identify the assumptions in your problem statement, to reframe the question from five different stakeholder perspectives, and to articulate what you might be missing. This upstream AI use is often more valuable than using AI to generate solution ideas.
source_ref: Stanford University's Jeremy Utley. AI's brainstorming and problem-solving potential — video

content: Utley teaches that the quality of a question is often a direct proxy for the quality of the thinking that produced it. Vague questions generate vague ideas; precise, human-centered, tension-filled questions generate ideas that are specific enough to actually test. Learning to craft a great question is therefore a metacognitive skill — it forces you to articulate what you know, what you don't know, and what tension you are trying to resolve.
implication: Make question quality a visible, reviewable artifact. Before a brainstorm, write the problem statement on a whiteboard and invite the room to improve it. Treat a better question as a meaningful deliverable — not just a warm-up.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley and Perry Klebahn | Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters | Talks at Google — video

content: The 'How Might We' construction is deliberately optimistic in its grammar: 'how' assumes a solution exists, 'might' removes the pressure of finding the only solution, and 'we' signals that it is a collective challenge. Each word in the formulation does work. Changing any one of them — to 'how can we' or 'how should we' — subtly narrows the creative aperture in ways that matter at scale.
implication: Train teams on the anatomy of a 'How Might We' question so they can diagnose when their question is inadvertently constraining the solution space. The linguistic precision of the question is not pedantry — it is creative architecture.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar — Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care?

content: Utley frequently observes that the most innovative organizations are distinguished not by their ability to generate better solutions, but by their ability to notice better problems. Problem-noticing — the capacity to see friction, unmet needs, or embedded assumptions that others have normalized — is the upstream competency that makes everything else possible. It is also one of the most trainable skills that most organizations never deliberately develop.
implication: Create structured problem-noticing practices inside your organization: customer shadowing sessions, friction logs, assumption audits. Reward people who surface a better problem, not just people who deliver a polished solution to the problem they were handed.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley — podcast

content: The 'Wonder Wander' — a deliberate practice of taking a problem out into the physical world and colliding it with unrelated stimuli — is partly a problem-reframing tool. When you carry a question into a new environment, you are forced to see it through different lenses: a nail salon becomes a model for upselling, a traffic light becomes a scarcity signal. The environment reshapes the problem, not just the solutions.
implication: Assign a 'Wonder Wander' before finalizing a problem statement — not just to generate ideas, but to test whether the framing holds up when viewed from radically different contexts. If the problem looks different from every angle, you may not yet have found its stable core.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: Utley argues that the most dangerous moment in any innovation process is when a team mistakes confidence in their problem statement for correctness. Early confidence in a framing shuts down the exploratory posture that better framing requires. Building in deliberate skepticism about the problem statement — even after the team feels they have it right — is a structural safeguard against the most expensive kind of innovation failure.
implication: Institutionalize a 'problem statement challenge' step: after a team has agreed on their problem framing, assign one person the role of interrogating it. Have them argue that the real problem is different, that the framing contains hidden assumptions, or that solving this problem would not actually produce the outcome the team wants.
source_ref: Episode 259 — Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley — podcast

content: Utley connects great question design to the idea that creativity is fundamentally about making connections across frames of reference. A well-designed question is one that deliberately creates productive tension between two frames — the user's experience and the business constraint, the current reality and the desired future, the technical limit and the human need. That tension is not a problem with the question; it is the generative engine inside it.
implication: When crafting a problem statement, explicitly name the tension the question holds — who or what is in conflict, and what makes this hard. A question with no embedded tension is likely a question whose answer is already known, which means it does not need innovation; it needs execution.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: Utley observes that most brainstorming meetings implicitly begin with a problem statement that was written by one person, under time pressure, before the meeting started — and then treated as fixed throughout the session. This single structural habit is responsible for a large fraction of innovation failures, because it means the team never had permission to question whether they were working on the right problem in the first place.
implication: Make the opening of every brainstorm a problem-statement workshop, not a problem-statement presentation. The question should be co-created or at minimum interrogated by the full group before any solution generation begins. The ten minutes spent doing this will save hours of misaligned ideation.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley — podcast

content: Utley teaches that narrow problem framing is often a form of hidden risk aversion. When a team frames a problem very tightly — constraining the scope, the user, the acceptable solution types — they are often unconsciously managing anxiety about openness rather than accurately describing the problem. Recognizing this pattern is the first step toward asking genuinely ambitious questions.
implication: When a problem statement feels 'safe' or immediately actionable, treat that as a signal that it may be too narrow. Ask the team: what are we ruling out by framing it this way, and are we ruling it out because we know it's irrelevant or because it feels uncomfortable to include?
source_ref: Zero to One: The Myth That Keeps Individuals, Teams, and Organizations from Breakthrough Innovation — video

content: Utley draws a direct line between the quality of empathy research and the quality of problem definition. Teams that spend more time genuinely understanding users — observing behavior, listening for unarticulated needs, noticing what people do rather than what they say — consistently produce sharper, more generative problem statements. Surface-level research produces surface-level questions; deep human understanding produces questions that open unexpected doors.
implication: Do not allow a problem statement to be finalized until the team has direct contact with the people experiencing the problem. Secondhand research, internal assumptions, and market data alone produce problem statements that reflect what the team already believed — not what the user actually needs.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar — Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care?

content: Utley frequently uses the language of 'flaring' and 'focusing' to describe the two modes of creative work — and problem finding is fundamentally a flaring activity. Before narrowing in on a single problem definition, teams should generate multiple candidate problem statements, explore adjacent framings, and allow the question space to expand before it contracts. The discipline is not premature focus but disciplined expansion followed by deliberate convergence.
implication: Treat the problem-finding phase with the same diverge-then-converge structure you would apply to ideation. Generate ten possible problem framings before committing to one. The version you started with may not survive contact with the alternatives — and that is the point.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Utley connects the scarcity of good problem-finders to the way creativity is typically taught — or not taught. Because most educational and professional environments reward people for having right answers quickly, the skill of dwelling productively in uncertainty, holding open questions, and resisting premature resolution is systematically undertrained. Great problem-finders are not geniuses; they are people who have built the tolerance for ambiguity that others abandoned early.
implication: Build ambiguity tolerance explicitly into innovation programs. Assign problems with no clear answer and reward the quality of the questions asked, not the speed of resolution. This is the competency that compounds over a career and cannot be downloaded from a framework.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: AI Ignites Human Creativity — video

content: Utley points out that asking AI to help interrogate a problem framing — to steelman the question, identify its hidden assumptions, and propose alternative framings — is one of the highest-ROI uses of AI in the innovation process. Most people ask AI to help them once they have already decided what the problem is; the greater leverage comes from using AI before that decision is made, as a rigorous thinking partner on the question itself.
implication: Before finalizing any problem statement, feed it to an AI system and ask it to: (1) identify assumptions embedded in the framing, (2) restate the problem from three different stakeholder perspectives, and (3) propose a more ambitious version of the same question. Use its output not as the answer but as a pressure-test on your framing.
source_ref: AI as Your New Strategic Thought Partner with Jeremy Utley, Stanford, Ideaflow, & Beyond the Prompt — video

content: Utley observes that the organizations most likely to solve the wrong problem are those with the most capable execution cultures. High-competence organizations are rewarded so consistently for delivering against defined briefs that they develop a structural bias against questioning the brief itself. The very capabilities that make them excellent executors make them poor problem-finders — and the switch from one mode to the other requires deliberate structural intervention, not just cultural encouragement.
implication: In high-performance organizations, create protected spaces — specific teams, specific sessions, specific leadership postures — where the normal execution norms are explicitly suspended, and questioning the problem is not just permitted but required. Without structural permission, cultural capability will always default to execution.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization — interview
